questions.question: What is the appropriate scope for creative self-help practices taught in this course?

options.text: Creative practices can treat and cure all mental health conditions
Creative self-help is appropriate for mild-to-moderate distress and as complement to clinical care—NOT substitute for treatment of severe conditions
Creative expression should replace therapy and medication for all conditions
Creative practices have no therapeutic value and shouldn't be used

questions.explanation: Creative self-help is appropriate for mild-to-moderate stress/anxiety/low mood and as complement to (not replacement for) clinical care. It is NOT sufficient for persistent suicidal ideation, severe depression, active psychosis, acute trauma, severe eating disorders, or other clinical-level conditions requiring specialized treatment. Use creative practices alongside professional care when needed.

questions.question: Why does anxiety need containment and predictability in creative practices?

options.text: Anxious people can't handle any creative freedom
Anxiety is reduced by grounding techniques with clear boundaries (time limits, structured prompts, repetitive patterns)—open-ended tasks can increase overwhelm
All creative work for anxiety must be identical and follow rigid rules
Containment doesn't matter—any creative activity helps anxiety equally

questions.explanation: Anxiety needs containment because open-ended, ambiguous tasks can increase overwhelm. Effective anti-anxiety techniques include: time-limited worry journaling (10 min, then close), repetitive pattern drawing (grounding through rhythm), body mapping (somatically grounding), structured prompts. Predictability and boundaries help rather than adding to anxiety.

questions.question: What is the appropriate creative approach for moderate-to-severe depression?

options.text: Demand elaborate creative projects to 'snap out of it'
Micro-tasks (5 min max), very low-barrier activities, and acknowledge that severe depression needs professional treatment—creative work is complement, not cure
Force gratitude journaling even if it feels invalidating
Avoid all creative work until completely better

questions.explanation: Moderate-to-severe depression requires professional treatment (therapy, medication). If engaging creatively during depression: use micro-tasks (5 min max), very low-barrier activities (coloring, one-sentence journaling), no judgment if you can't do more. Severe depression is a medical condition—creative practices complement treatment but don't replace it. Any tiny engagement is success, not failure.

questions.question: What is the recommended sequence for processing anger through creative practices?

options.text: Suppress anger completely and never express it
Physical discharge (safe containers like pounding clay, tearing paper) → reflective journaling → visual expression with containment
Explosive venting without any containment or reflection
Immediately journal about anger without any physical release

questions.explanation: Healthy anger processing sequence: (1) Physical discharge in safe containers (pound clay, tear paper, hit pillows, energetic movement) to release physiological activation; (2) Reflective journaling to process (what happened, how I feel, what I need, what's in my control); (3) Visual expression with containment if needed. Pure venting without reflection can increase rumination; suppression doesn't work either.

questions.question: When should you seek professional help instead of relying solely on creative self-help?

options.text: Whenever you feel any difficult emotion
If experiencing persistent suicidal thoughts, severe functional impairment, uncontrolled rage, traumatic grief, or clinical-level symptoms
Never—creative self-help is always sufficient
Only if you want someone to tell you what your art means

questions.explanation: Seek professional help for: persistent suicidal thoughts (even without plan), severe depression preventing functioning, uncontrolled rage causing harm, traumatic grief, panic attacks disrupting life, or any clinical-level symptoms. Creative self-help complements clinical care but cannot replace specialized treatment for moderate-to-severe conditions. Crisis resources: 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline) or text HOME to 741741.
